# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Sam Neill has passed away at the age of 78.

**Score:** 14009

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|d10dMmzqCYqQ0)  
Rip to a legend 
2. Everyone thinks of jurassic Park but I always think of his incredible performance in event horizon. 

Rip
  - In the mouth of madness is his best for me 
    - https://i.redd.it/ljfpw3iboych1.gif
3. Loved him in peaky blinders. RIP Inspector Campbell. 
4. ![gif](giphy|iHE8cpchZEjLbaRKzu)

nooooooooooooooooooooooo
5. My man... ☹️ 
  - His initial reaction in Jurassic Park to seeing a dinosaur is amazing.

https://i.redd.it/1ybenhcp6ych1.gif

He really helps sell the wonder of it.
    - That wasn't acting, Dr Grant actually saw a dinosaur. I know this because this was my childhood.
6. How is he 78?! God I’m old

He is a legend 

## 2. Harrison Ford turns 84 today. Happy birthday to him!

**Score:** 1905

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This picture gives off the vibes that Harrison Ford was just cast as the new James Bond. 
2. And Patrick Stewart turns 86!
3. I hope he gets to punch a Nazi today!
4. HB to my favourite elderly stoner. 
5. ![gif](giphy|XvjC06Gh9lhfZNBNIM)
6. Happy Birthday to the one and only Harrison Ford
7. The fact that he played some of the most iconic characters of all time - Han Solo & Indiana Jones is just extraordinary
  - Fun fact: Harrison is older than Ian McDiarmid who played Emperor Palpatine 
8. Pot head of the century! 
9. Legend.

## 3. RIP, Sam Neill. You will be missed.

**Score:** 1698

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorite actors and always seemed to be a decent person behind the fame. I hate this news.
  - He always carried himself with so much dignity

  - Hunt for red October,  
2. This is a GREAT Sam Neill movie

https://preview.redd.it/wx4ctv555zch1.jpeg?width=1079&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a62a445ab1eae566f526d88e7dedcc4d9604e0c5
3. Thank you, Dr. Grant. 
4. Rip Cardinal Wolsey
5. I don’t tear up easily, but this one got me. Rip Sam, thanks for the memories 🫡
6. Can’t say he had a bad role. Ever.   


https://preview.redd.it/gmo91w65xzch1.jpeg?width=1024&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6803478fe64c84c0f8e227deb2acd0a287dad334
7. How did he die ?
  - They haven't said, yet. He was in remission from blood cancer. 

## 4. X-Men: The Last Stand has plenty of flaws, but this scene is genuinely excellent. Ian McKellen was really good as Magneto and never missed with his performance.

**Score:** 1663

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I honestly preferred his exchange with Pyro, but yeah, McKellen never missed, not even by half a note

https://preview.redd.it/fbjpzq36txch1.jpeg?width=400&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=817b9019d0f40d73056ccc3e41762ea49078e649
  - Yeah 100% my pick. What a nuanced hero and villain relationship they have, it's fantastic
    - Even as a kid I loved this exchange 
2. "Guys, why the fuck didn't you tell me he's a holocaust survivor? I look really fucking stupid now."
3. That's the best scene. He isn't defined by whatever mark that they define themselves by. He just is and he doesn't need to show it off with any mark
4. Little known fact about Magneto is he is an anti vaxxer
5. And then Beast just shoves like 4 needles in his chest. What a dick
6. Good foreshadowing, too. Since the needle which is at the heart of the movie’s central conflict does indeed touch his skin in the end.
7. what mark is she talking about though ?
  - In that film they had tattoos representing being mutants - akin to the branding in the comics/cartoon, but for very different reasons

## 5. Whats the most hilariously unexpected ending you've seen in a comedic movie?

**Score:** 1106
**Body:** The image is from the movie "Monty Python And The Holy Grail" btw.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blazing Saddles

![img](9F17691B-FD5A-4E46-9001-4701B8EB5F10)
  - “I’m workin fer Mel Brooks!” 
2. It's extremely hard to beat, "Well, nobody's perfect!" from *Some Like It Hot* (1959).
3. ![gif](giphy|5nPodXMLXXd1m)
4. That's not even the most unexpected Python scene IMO :)

https://preview.redd.it/quqbhl5t8zch1.png?width=480&format=png&auto=webp&s=8c73545e739c37fefd618ee4712d48b68ad9f795

  

5. ![gif](giphy|WZSqZcPQwlX20)  
“Oh Frank! Everyone should have a friend like you!”
6. Little Miss Sunshine's Dance

![gif](giphy|d3ML95ybDSnCisne)
  - Also Napoleon dynamites dance 
7. ![gif](giphy|l4EoT1vyOxj7zwwAU)  
Does this count as a comedy? If so… “don’t touch it! It’s evil!”
8. The age of Aquarius ending in 40 year old virgin

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. My Honest Thoughts on Biopics

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Most biopic movies fail to be good movies or effective portrayals of real life people.

\\#1 If it's very entertaining and well made as a movie, it's also very inaccurate.

\\#2 You can't capture an entire person's life story in 2 hours. Even 3 hours is not enough. Many significant people are removed from the life story portrayed in the movie.

A TV show biopic can work. Although it is difficult. Because you need many episodes and multiple seasons to explore a person's life, their character, and the things that happened to them. The audience needs time to understand why people are who they are and why they are that way.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Biopics that try to cover a person’s whole life or career are overstuffed and underdeveloped, like they used the subject’s Wikipedia page as a script. They *can* be well done when they focus on a specific event. 
2. "Most biopic movies fail to be good movies or effective portrayals of real life people."

This ignores the existence of a lot of good biopics that work as movies. Sure, you can't get into the weeds in two hours. But you can tell a good story. I do agree that the modern approach of focusing on pivotal periods as opposed to a cradle-to-the-grave approach is often more appropriate. Also, you often have the same problem adapting novels to screen.
  - Exactly; a good biopic is one that focuses on a pivotal moment in the protagonist's life—though that moment can span several years—which is precisely what turns them into a cinematic character. A vehicle for a theme.
3. They’re wack in general with the occasional exception, they tend to lionize or airbrush the central character and often follow an extremely formulaic rise and fall and redeem arc. Hollywood biopics pretty much just act as bids for the Best Actor/Actress award.
4. Your terms are arbitrary. A "successful" biopic does not need to capture the entirety of someone's life, it does not have to be accurate, it does not need to be an entertaining rump. I'm sure you have something like Michael (2026) in mind, and the genre does attract these moronic money-grabs, but there's a whole range of movies demonstrating that, of course, a good biopic is possible: Lawrence of Arabia, Raging Bull, The Elephant Man, Andrej Rublev, Spartacus...
5. I think they often contain a certain inherent contradiction. A person's life is not a moral tale, and reducing their experiences down to a lesson which can be understood by the audience is, in my opinion, vaguely offensive. On the other hand, if a traditional narrative arc isn't presented in which the hero overcomes a struggle, there's no catharsis for the audience and it's just two hours of random things happening.

Basically, it's extremely difficult to not editorialize or moralize a person's life, but doing so is a strange thing to do to a real, living person who I think deserves the dignity of not being so essentialized.
  - Thats a fantastic observation!


Beyond the grand moral arc I think even just in the minutae this will highly fuck you up as the filmmaker. Even just making up e.g. some interesting side character to make this or that scene work would to me seem kind of intrusive and unethical. You are intruding into this persons life and distorting them into some nice, slick narrative.


Going by your observation it also makes sense why reversely it works so well if you got something like the 8th Mile. If its Eminem himself editorializing his experiences  he is free to narrativize it as much as he wants to to create a poignant moral arc.

---
